plainMeaning: I bow at the feet of eight Jinas who are yet to come — Pottila, Śatakīrti, Munisuvrata, Amama, Niṣkaṣāya, Niṣpulāka, Nirmama and Citragupta.
recitation: poṭila satakīrti, munisuvrata amama niḥkaṣāya | nipulāyaka nirmama, citragupti vandūṁ pāya ||

wordByWord.gloss: Pottila — a Jina yet to arise (9th of the future twenty-four)
wordByWord.etymology: Proper name; appears as Poṭila / Pottila / Proṣṭhila in the lists of the anāgata caubīsī; exact vyutpatti uncertain.
wordByWord.notes: First of the eight future Jinas named in this verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śata 'hundred' + kīrti 'fame, glory'; the book spells it satakīrti (without ś-).
muni 'sage' + su 'good' + vrata 'vow'; the same name as the 20th Jina of the present caubīsī, reused here for a future Jina.
a- (privative) + mama 'mine'; a speaking-name evoking freedom from mamatva (possessive attachment).
wordByWord.notes: Some Śvetāmbara lists place Amama at position 13 after an intervening 'Sarvavid'; this hymn names it directly after Munisuvrata.

wordByWord.etymology: niḥ 'without' + kaṣāya 'passion' (krodha, māna, māyā, lobha — anger, pride, deceit, greed).
Skt. niṣpulāka: niḥ 'without' + pulāka 'chaff, blighted grain, flaw'; the book's reading nipulāyaka is a vernacular form.
wordByWord.notes: Standard lists read Niṣpulāka; the verse preserves nipulāyaka verbatim.

wordByWord.etymology: nir 'without' + mama 'mine'; paired in sense with Amama above.
Skt. citra 'wondrous, manifold' + gupti 'guarding, restraint'; the book's caitya-vandana form citragupti for the name Citragupta.
wordByWord.notes: Eighth and last future Jina named in this verse (16th of the future twenty-four).

wordByWord.etymology: From √vand 'to bow, praise, venerate'; old Gujarati / Apabhraṁśa first-person form 'I bow'.
wordByWord.notes: The single verb that governs all eight names of the verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit / Apabhraṁśa pāya for Skt. pāda 'foot' (Paia-sadda-mahannavo: paya = pāda).
wordByWord.notes: Object of vandūṁ: 'I bow to (their) feet.'

literalTranslation: Pottila, Śatakīrti, Munisuvrata, Amama, Niṣkaṣāya; Niṣpulāka, Nirmama, Citragupta — (their) feet I bow to.
idiomaticTranslation: To Pottila and Śatakīrti, to Munisuvrata, Amama and Niṣkaṣāya, to Niṣpulāka, Nirmama and Citragupta — at the feet of these eight Jinas-to-come I bow.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse simply continues the roll-call of the anāgata caubīsī begun just before: where the preceding verse named the first eight of the coming Jinas (Padmanābha down to Pedhāla), this one names the next eight — numbers nine through sixteen — and the following verse will close the set with Samādhi onward. A single verb, vandūṁ pāya ('I bow to the feet'), is held back to the very end and made to govern all eight names at once, so that the whole line is one sustained act of homage. What rewards a closer ear is that these are 'speaking names'. Four of the eight are built on a privative prefix and read almost as a litany of liberation itself: Amama and Nirmama ('free of mine-ness'), Niṣkaṣāya ('free of the passions'), Niṣpulāka ('without flaw or chaff'). To recite them is therefore to name, one after another, the very qualities a vītarāga embodies — absence of possessiveness, absence of the four kaṣāyas, absence of any inner blemish. The remaining names sound the positive note: Śatakīrti (boundless fame), Munisuvrata (perfect vows), Citragupta (wondrous self-guarding). The verse thus does double duty: it venerates particular future Tīrthaṅkaras and, through their names, quietly rehearses the marks of the goal toward which the worshipper bends.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Bowing to Jinas not yet born expresses a distinctly Jain conviction: the tīrtha is perpetual, and awakened guides arise endlessly across the turning ages, so reverence is owed not only to the historically-arisen but to those still to come. The cluster of privative names — niṣkaṣāya, amama, nirmama, niṣpulāka — encodes the doctrinal core of Tīrthaṅkara-hood: the vītarāga state, won by extinguishing the kaṣāyas (anger, pride, deceit, greed) and the mamatva (possessive 'mine-making') that bind the soul to saṁsāra. Bowing specifically to the pāya (feet) is the standard Jain idiom of self-surrender, laying one's ego at the lowest part of the venerated body.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at dawn, each privative name can be taken as a small resolve for the day: to loosen possessiveness (amama, nirmama) and to keep anger and greed in check (niṣkaṣāya). The reciter venerates the future Jinas while inwardly naming the qualities to cultivate before they ever arise.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava): the parallel naming-veneration of the present caubīsī of twenty-four Jinas
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgaccha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Anāgata caubīsī (future twenty-four), Śvetāmbara enumeration (Pottila … Citragupta as nos. 9–16)
crossReferences.source: Jain tradition as recorded in Jain Rūp Maṇḍan (jainqq.org)

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: The concept of a caubīsī of twenty-four Jinas per half-cycle and the future (utsarpiṇī) set, and the vītarāga / kaṣāya-free nature of a Jina.

sources.title: Future twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras (Anāgata Caubīsī) — Śvetāmbara list
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the names and order Pottila, Śatakīrti, Munisuvrata, Amama, Niṣkaṣāya, Niṣpulāka, Nirmama, Citragupta as positions 9–16.

sources.title: Paia-sadda-mahannavo (Sheth), Prakrit–Hindi dictionary, via Cologne/DSAL & Wisdomlib
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa paya = Skt. pāda ('foot') and the root √vand for vandūṁ.

sources.title: Paia-sadda-mahannavo (Sheth), full dictionary
sources.consultedFor: Corroborating the Prakrit glosses of pāya and the privative-prefix name-elements (niṣ-, a-, nir-).

notes: The eight names are future Tīrthaṅkaras nos. 9–16 by this hymn's own reckoning. The book's orthography is preserved verbatim: satakīrti (for Śatakīrti), nipulāyaka (for the standard Niṣpulāka), citragupti (for Citragupta), and the Apabhraṁśa pāya (for pāda). Standard Śvetāmbara lists sometimes insert 'Sarvavid' between Munisuvrata and Amama, shifting the numbering; this hymn omits it, so the count here follows the hymn rather than those lists — flagged as a variant. Etymological readings of the names (Amama, Nirmama, Niṣkaṣāya, Niṣpulāka) as 'speaking names' are transparent Sanskrit derivations; the proper name Poṭila has no securely attested etymology and is left unglossed.
